MD, Sofronio Sagucio STORY OF PRESENT LLNESS: This is a case of a 66-year-old male with a significant past medical history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, prior cerebrovascular accident with residual left-sided deficits, peripheral arterial disease, and chronic urinary obstruction secondary to prostate hyperplasia, who presented to the hospital on 2/19/2026 via EMS for inability to care for himself and concern for multiple infected wounds. According to available documentation and EMS report, the patient was found sitting in his wheelchair covered in stool and urine, raising concerns regarding severe functional decline and inability to performactivities of daily living independently. The patient reportedly lives alone at home and is wheel bound at baseline due to residual deficits froma prior CVA approximately five years ago, including persistent left-sided weakness and left upper extremity contracture. On initial evaluation in the energency department, the patient was noted to have multiple wounds, ncluding a large infected wound of the left thigh and an ulcerated wound along the lateral aspect of the right foot, with associated soft-tissue swelling and concern for developing osteomyelitis. Initial I aboratory evaluation demonstrated eukocytosis, anemia, elevated creatinine, elevated CK, elevated CRP, and mildly elevated lactic acid. Respiratory viral testing was negative. Imaging studies revealed a large rimenhancing fluid collection measuring approximately 13.9 X 10.7 cmalong the lateral aspect of the left thigh concerning for abscess formation with internal air suggesting possible comunication with the skin or necrotizing infection. Imaging also demonstrated moderate to severe bilateral hydronephrosis and adder distention with marked omegaly consistent with chronic urinary outlet obstruction. Lower extremity vascular studies showed advanced peripheral arterial sease absent flowin the right posterior tibial artery and monophasi c distal waveforms consistent with atherosclerosis. The patient was initially treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics and admitted for further management.
